coredata.dc:title: The effect of 1 MeV electron irradiation on charge carrier localization in graphene quantum dot array of reduced graphene oxide
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Rudenko, R.M.
Voitsihovska, O.O.
Abakumov, A.A.
Bychko, I.B.
Poroshin, V.N.
coredata.dc:description:
This paper presents a study of the effect of 1 MeV electron irradiation on the localization of charge carriers in graphene quantum dots (GQDs) located in a reduced graphene oxide (RGO). The electrical properties were analyzed in a wide temperature range of 4.2–340 K for fluences of 0–160 × 1015 cm−2. The localization length, dielectric permittivity, and density of localized states were estimated. It was found that the conductivity and charge localization parameters exhibit non-monotonic behavior depending on the electron fluences. It can change several times in the vicinity of a fluence of 10 × 1015 cm−2 compared to the higher or lower fluence. It is shown that electron irradiation affects the disordered surrounding matrix, which leads to an increase in the content of oxygen and functional groups. At the same time, the size of the sp
2 ‑carbon domains does not depend on the fluence. It is assumed that electron irradiation indirectly affects the properties of GQDs. The transformation of the surrounding disordered matrix leads to a modification of the electronic properties of the GQDs array and the RGO as a whole. The non-monotonic behavior of the RGO conductivity upon irradiation is due to the features of the energy spectrum of the GQDs array, which is characterized by the presence of periodic peaks in the density of states. The irradiation-induced structural modification likely leads to a shift in the Fermi level and a change in the density of states. It is shown that the RGO behaves like a wide-band doped semiconductor. The difference is that the impurity level was formed by GQDs. At temperatures above 310 K, the RGO conductivity exhibits an Arrhenius behavior with an activation energy of about 60 meV due to charge carrier delocalization at thermal ejection into the extended band.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Reduced graphene oxide
Electron beam irradiation
Electrical properties
Quantum dot array

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of 1 MeV electron irradiation on the structure of the RGO
Electrical conductivity of RGO at temperatures above 100 K
Behavior of density of states in the vicinity of Fermi level under electron irradiation

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluence dependences of conductivity measured at different temperatures for irradiated RGO.
Temperature (a) and field (b) dependences of conductivity for pristine and irradiated RGO.

Fluence dependences of the characteristic temperature T
0 and the characteristic electric field E
0 (a), localization length ξ, and dielectric permittivity ε (b).

XPS spectra C1s (a), O1s(b), N1s(c) for pristine and irradiated RGO. Fluence dependences of the molar ratios C/O, C/(O + N), and the sp
2 ‑carbon fraction in the RGO (d).

(a) Raman spectra recorded on pristine and irradiated RGO. (b) Fluence dependences of FWHM of G-band and sp
2 ‑carbon crystallite sizes.

(a) Typical behavior of temperature dependence of reduced activation energy W of RGO before and after electron irradiation. (b) Dependence of RGO conductivity on 1000/T in the temperature range of 300–340 K.

Fluence dependence of activation energy E
a .

Fluence dependence of Coulomb gap ∆
C (a) and density of states N
F in the vicinity of the Fermi level outside the Coulomb gap.

head.ce:title: The effect of 1 MeV electron irradiation on charge carrier localization in graphene quantum dot array of reduced graphene oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Physics, National Academy of Sciences, Kyiv 03680, Ukraine
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Physics, National Academy of Sciences, Kyiv 03680, Ukraine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: L.V. Pisarzhevskii Institute of Physical Chemistry, National Academy of Sciences, Kyiv 03028, Ukraine

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: L.V. Pisarzhevskii Institute of Physical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: L.V. Pisarzhevskii Institute of Physical Chemistry, National Academy of Sciences, Kyiv, 03028, Ukraine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
This paper presents a study of the effect of 1 MeV electron irradiation on the localization of charge carriers in graphene quantum dots (GQDs) located in a reduced graphene oxide (RGO). The electrical properties were analyzed in a wide temperature range of 4.2–340 K for fluences of 0–160 × 1015 cm−2. The localization length, dielectric permittivity, and density of localized states were estimated. It was found that the conductivity and charge localization parameters exhibit non-monotonic behavior depending on the electron fluences. It can change several times in the vicinity of a fluence of 10 × 1015 cm−2 compared to the higher or lower fluence. It is shown that electron irradiation affects the disordered surrounding matrix, which leads to an increase in the content of oxygen and functional groups. At the same time, the size of the sp
2 ‑carbon domains does not depend on the fluence. It is assumed that electron irradiation indirectly affects the properties of GQDs. The transformation of the surrounding disordered matrix leads to a modification of the electronic properties of the GQDs array and the RGO as a whole. The non-monotonic behavior of the RGO conductivity upon irradiation is due to the features of the energy spectrum of the GQDs array, which is characterized by the presence of periodic peaks in the density of states. The irradiation-induced structural modification likely leads to a shift in the Fermi level and a change in the density of states. It is shown that the RGO behaves like a wide-band doped semiconductor. The difference is that the impurity level was formed by GQDs. At temperatures above 310 K, the RGO conductivity exhibits an Arrhenius behavior with an activation energy of about 60 meV due to charge carrier delocalization at thermal ejection into the extended band.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para:
Reduced graphene oxide (RGO) has been the subject of intense research for optical and electrical applications for many years [1–10]. The unique properties of RGO are determined by its structure containing sp
2 and sp
3 ‑carbon, as well as various functional groups and defects [1–5]. It is possible to adjust the properties of the material over a wide range by changing the ratio of sp
2 and sp
3 ‑carbon, as well as varying the content of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups that can dope or withdraw electric charge carriers [1,2,4,6,9,11–16]. Despite the researcher's efforts, the properties of the RGO are far from fully understood. The unique structure, on the one hand, provides unique properties, but on the other hand, the disorder present complicates the study, simulation, and prediction of the RGO properties.

The significant feature of the RGO materials is that the regions of crystalline sp
2 ‑carbons act like quantum dots [3,6,8,15,17]. Thus, RGO is a unique material in which an array of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is formed naturally during synthesis [8]. The array properties depend not only on the characteristics of quantum dots but also on the properties of the matrix in which they are immersed [18–20]. The sp
3 ‑carbon, defects, and functional groups form the surrounding matrix and interface states, and they also affect the properties of the material [3,21,22]. It should be noted that, in general, a quantum dot can be considered an artificial atom [18–20,23]. Quantum dots, like atoms, have a discrete energy spectrum. The creation of molecules or even semiconductor materials with an extended conduction band is possible by combining quantum dots [23–27]. Quantum dots immersed in an insulating matrix can act like impurities, creating localized states in the bandgap [18–20]. Studies show that the optical and electrical properties of RGO in the nano- and macroscales can be successfully described by the model of a disordered array of quantum dots [3,6,17,28]. The GQDs in the RGO structure should probably be understood as areas consisting of 3–5 layers of sp
2 ‑carbon with a total thickness of ∼1 nm and a lateral size of about d ≈ 3÷8 nm [3,17,29,30].

It was previously found [9,13] that electron irradiation with a fluence of 1 × 1015–170 × 1015 cm−2 leads to a pronounced non-monotonic change in the mechanical and electrical properties of the RGO by several times. It is assumed [9] that such behavior is associated with structural changes and the formation of additional sp
3 ‑carbon bonds between sp
2 -domains. In other words, it is due to the transformation of the isolate matrix that surrounds the GQDs. As the temperature decreases, the changes in electrical conductivity under electron irradiation become more pronounced [13]. At low temperatures (below 100 K), electric charge transfer occurs due to tunneling between localized impurity states in the band gap. The localized states involved in conduction are likely created by an array of GQDs [3,17,28,31]. Therefore, the enhancement of the non-monotonic behavior of the RGO properties with decreasing temperatures is probably due to the direct or indirect effect of electron irradiation on the properties of an array of graphene quantum dots. By indirect effect, it should be understood that the properties of quantum dots also depend on the properties of the surrounding matrix, which are sensitive to irradiation. It is assumed [3,17,28,31] that in RGO at low temperatures, charge carriers are localized in GQDs, and charge transfer occurs due to tunneling through the barriers created by the insulating matrix. The features of charge localization are characterized by the localization length ξ and the effective dielectric permittivity ε. At low temperatures, charge transfer in the RGO follows the hopping conduction (VRH) mechanism by the Efros-Shklovskii model (ESVRH) [19,28,32]. It allows the estimate of the localization length ξ and permittivity ε in the irradiated material from the study of the temperature and field dependences of the conductivity [17,19,28].

In line with the above said, this work is concentrated on the study of the effect of 1 MeV electron irradiation on the localization of charge carriers in an array of GQDs in the fluence range where a nonmonotonic behavior of the RGO properties is observed. According to the ESVRH model, the temperature dependence of the conductivity σ follows the expression [17,19,28]:
(1)
σ =
σ 0
⋅ exp
−
T 0
T
1 / 2
where σ
0 is the temperature-independent prefactor, Т
0 is the characteristic temperature. The characteristic temperature is related to the charge localization length ξ and the permittivity ε by the equation:
(2)
T 0
=
2.8
e 2
4
πεε 0
k B
ξ
where ε
0 is the permittivity of vacuum, k
B is the Boltzmann constant, and e is the electron charge.

According to the ESVRH conductivity mechanism, the energy required for the tunneling charge carriers between localized states can be provided by the applied electric field [19]. At liquid helium temperatures and sufficiently high electric field, field-driven hopping conductivity follows the expression:
(3)
σ ∝ exp
−
E 0
E
1 2
where E
0 is the characteristic electric field. It is related to the characteristic temperature T
0 by the following expression:
(4)
E 0
=
2
k B
T 0
eξ

Therefore, the ESVRH model makes it possible to determine the localization length and permittivity in the initial and irradiated RGO using expressions (1)–(4) and the experimentally obtained data on the characteristic temperature T
0 and the characteristic electric field E
0 .

The preparation of studied RGO is described in our previous works [13,17]. Briefly, the precursor GO was synthesized using the modified Hummers method, and the reduction was carried out with hydrazine-hydrate. The samples were irradiated at room temperature in the air by 1 MeV electrons with fluences 1 × 1015, 4 × 1015, 10 × 1015, 20 × 1015, 40 × 1015, 100 × 1015, and 160 × 1015 cm−2. The thickness of pristine and irradiated RGO is ∼3 μm. The electrical measurements were carried out in the helium cryostat in the temperature range of 4.2–340 K. Raman spectra were collected on the Horiba Jobin Yvon spectrometer using 2.54 eV (488 nm) at room temperatures. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on JPS-9200 (JEOL) spectrometer with monochromatic irradiation MgKα source (10 kV, 15 mkA).

Fig. 1 shows the fluence dependences of the conductivity of irradiated RGO measured at different temperatures. The non-monotonic behavior becomes more pronounced as the measurement temperature decreases. It is clear in the vicinity of the fluence Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2, a conductivity peak is observed, which is replaced by a minimum with a subsequent increase in conductivity. In the study [9], the conductivity peak was observed at a fluence of 20 × 1015 cm−2. The shift towards higher fluences is probably due to 10 MeV electron irradiation and an argon atmosphere in [9], in contrast to the current work, where we used 1 MeV irradiation in air. Despite the peak shift, the conductivity behavior is qualitatively the same in both cases. Irradiation with 500 keV protons also caused the non-monotonic behavior of the conductivity [21,22]. It should be noted that not only the peak of conductivity is observed in [9] at the fluence Φ = 20 × 1015 cm−2, but also there are peaks for hardness and tensile strength. It causes considerable interest to study the electrical properties and structure of the RGO in the vicinity of the fluence Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2 after electron irradiation.

Fig. 2a and b show the temperature and electric-field dependences of the conductivity for pristine and irradiated RGO on the plots lnσ vs T
-1/2 and lnσ vs E
-1/2 . The temperature dependences reveal the linear behavior of the experimental data at temperatures below 100 K. At temperatures higher, there is a conductivity deviation from the behavior described by expression (1). The high-temperature behavior will be discussed below (see part 3.3). The linear behavior of the temperature dependences at temperatures below 100 K (Fig. 2a) and the linear behavior of the conductivity at 4.2 K in the region of sufficiently high electric fields (Fig. 2b) confirm the ESVRH mechanism in the pristine and irradiated RGO. The characteristic temperature T
0 and the characteristic field E
0 were determined from the slope of the linear parts of the experimental data on the plots lnσ vs T
-1/2 and lnσ vs E
-1/2 , respectively.

The values of the characteristic temperature T
0 and the electric field E
0 also show a non-monotonic behavior with fluence increase (Fig. 3a). In the vicinity of the fluence Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2, where a pronounced conductivity peak is observed (Fig. 1), the parameters T
0 and E
0 have the significant changes. In addition, at a fluence of Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2, they exhibit the opposite behavior, i.e., the characteristic temperature decreases while the characteristic field increases. At other studied fluences Φ, the parameters T
0 and E
0 simultaneously show an increase or decrease. The obtained values of T
0 , E
0 , and expressions (2) and (4) allow us to calculate the localization length ξ and permittivity ε at different fluences. The calculated values of localization length and dielectric permittivity under irradiation are within the intervals ξ ≈ 5÷7 nm and ε ≈ 5÷7, except for the vicinity of the fluence Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2 (Fig. 3b). The values ξ ≈ 5÷7 nm and ε ≈ 5÷7 are in agreement with known research data [3, 8, 28, 31, etc.]. At the fluence of 10 × 1015 cm−2, the parameters ξ and ε change several times compared to higher or lower fluences. The localization length decreases to 1 nm, and the dielectric permittivity increases to 35. In other words, ionizing irradiation by some fluences may strongly affect the charge carrier localization, which can be the reason for the non-monotonic behavior of the RGO conductivity under irradiation.

It should be noted that an increase in permittivity is often observed in doped semiconductor materials on the insulator side at the approach to the insulator-metal transition (IMT). In this case, an increase in the permittivity is accompanied by an increase in the localization length. But in our case, the localization length demonstrates a different behavior. Namely, it decreases from ∼5 nm to ∼1 nm. Probably, the sharp changes in the ξ and ε in the fluence range of the non-monotonic behavior of the conductivity of the irradiated RGO are due to reasons, the understanding of which requires additional studies. To achieve this goal it was studied the XPS and Raman spectra recorded on the initial and irradiated RGO (see part 3.2), the conductivity at temperatures above 100 K (see part 3.3), and the density of states in the vicinity of the Fermi level was estimated (see part 3.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of 1 MeV electron irradiation on the structure of the RGO
ce:section.ce:para:
XPS are shown in Fig. 4 . The C1s spectra of pristine and irradiated materials were deconvoluted into six components (Fig. 4a) according to [33–36]: sp
2 ‑carbon (peak C1), defect/sp
3 ‑carbon (C2), CO bonds of hydroxyls and ether groups (C3), CO bonds of carbonyls (C4), C(O)OH carboxylic bonds (C5), and a π–π* transition peak (C6). There is a contribution of nitrogen bonds to the C2–C5 bands due to reduction with hydrazine-hydrate, which leads to nitrogen doping of the studied RGO [11,12,34]. The deconvolution of O1s spectra (Fig. 4b) shows lines of quinones (peak O1), CO (O2), and CO (O3) bonds [33,34]. The pyridines (peak N1), pyrroles (N2), graphitic nitrogen (N3), and little content of N-oxides (N4) present in the N1s spectra (Fig. 4c). Pseudo-Voigt functions were used for the C2–C6, O1–O3, N1–N4 bands and an asymmetric pseudo-Voigt function for C1. 20 % Lorentzian and 80 % Gaussian contributions were used for the C1–C6, O1–O3, and N1–N4 bands. An analysis of C1s, O1s, and N1s spectra intensities found the fluence dependence of molar ratios of C/O and C/(O + N) (Fig. 4d). A significant effect of irradiation on the nitrogen content was not revealed. The nitrogen percentage is about ∼2÷3 % for pristine and irradiated RGO. At the same time, irradiation leads to an increase in the percentage of oxygen from ∼10 % in the pristine material to ∼20 % after the maximum fluence. An increase in the oxygen content leads to a decrease in the ratios C/O and C/(O + N) from 9.2 and 6.7 to 3.4 and 3.0, respectively. The sp
2 ‑carbon fraction was calculated by multiplying the percentage of carbon deduced from C1s, O1s, and N1s spectra intensities by the percentage of the area under the C1-band in the C1s spectrum. XPS data revealed a decrease in sp
2 ‑carbon fractions from 60 % to 45 % under electron irradiation after the fluence of Φ = 160 × 1015 cm−2 (Fig. 4d). It should be noted that the significant changes in the C1s spectra deconvolution are observed in the increase in the contribution of the C3 band from ∼5 % to ∼10 % of the area under the spectrum. It is consistent with the slight increase in the area under the O3 band (Fig. 4b) after irradiation compared to the pristine material. The quantitative changes of other functional groups in the spectra of C1s, O1s, and N1s are too small and seem complicated to analyze.

As noted above, the band C3 at ∼285.5 eV corresponds to CO bonds in hydroxyls and ether groups [33–36]. These groups are impurities able to dope or withdraw electrons depending on the location at the edge or basal plan [37–39]. In any case, a change in the impurity content by several percent in the insulating matrix can cause a significant modification of the conductive properties of the irradiated RGO.

An investigation of the Raman spectra did not find significant changes in the RGO structure before and after irradiation. The Raman spectra (Fig. 5a) contain two major well-known bands, namely the D-band (∼1355 cm−1) and the G-band (∼1600 cm−1), and two minor, namely the D*-band (∼1150 cm−1) and D″-band (∼1500 cm−1) [40–42]. The latter two are probably related to polyacetylene at the grain boundaries [43]. The spectra are deconvoluted using two Lorentzian (D* and D″) and two asymmetric Pearson IV lines (D and G). The contributions of the D* and D″ bands are insignificant. The ID/IG ratio and width of bands in Raman spectra do not reveal significant changes under electron irradiation. Before and after irradiation ID/IG ratio is ∼1. The full width at half maximum of the G-band allowed us to estimate the average sizes of the sp
2 ‑carbon crystallites [17,44]. The monotonic dependence of the half-width at half-maximum of the G-band (Γ
G ) on the average size L
a of sp
2 -crystallites of carbon (2 nm < L
a < 10 nm) can be described by the expression [44]:
(5)
Γ G
=
68 ± 4
–
5.2 ± 0.5
∙
L a

It was found that the crystallite sizes are near 6 nm for all studied fluences, including the fluence Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2 (Fig. 5b). Thus, electron irradiation does not lead to a change in the average size of the sp
2 ‑carbon regions.

It is interesting to note that the final improvement in RGO conductivity at a fluence of Φ = 160 × 1015 cm−2 occurs with a simultaneous increase in the oxygen content (from ∼10 % to ∼20 %) and a decrease in the sp
2 ‑carbon fraction (from 60 % to 45 %). It is traditionally considered that the improvement in the conductivity of RGO is achieved by reducing the oxygen content and increasing the volume of the sp
2 ‑carbon fraction [16,28,31,39]. The role of oxygen and functional groups in the RGO is complex because it can both increase and decrease the conductivity.

Thus, studies of XPS and Raman data show that irradiation causes an increase in the oxygen content and a decrease of the sp
2 ‑carbon fraction at the unchanged average size of sp
2 ‑carbon crystallites. The increase in the oxygen content is accompanied by the rise in the amount of CO bonds of hydroxyls and ether groups from 5 % for the initial to 10 % for the irradiated RGO. It indicates the effect of electron irradiation on the insulating matrix. The mentioned groups can dope and withdraw charge carriers in the RGO [37–39], which may be the reason for the non-monotonic behavior of the conductivity after irradiation. The unchanged size of sp
2 ‑carbon crystallites with the changes in the content of oxygen and functional groups may indicate that electron irradiation primarily affects the disordered RGO matrix than sp
2 ‑carbon domains. Nevertheless, the effect on sp
2 ‑carbon may be indirect since changes in the properties of the surrounding insulating matrix affect the properties of quantum dots [3,21,22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical conductivity of RGO at temperatures above 100 K
ce:section.ce:para:
Additional information about the properties of the irradiated RGO can be obtained from the conductivity analysis at temperatures above 100 K, where hopping conduction ceases to be the dominant conduction channel. To study the temperature dependences, reduced activation energy W was used, which is a logarithmic derivative [17,28,32]:
(6)
W =
∂ ln σ
T
∂ ln T

Fig. 6a shows a typical temperature dependence of reduced activation energy in plot lnW vs lnT for the original and irradiated RGO. It is clear to see that at temperatures below 100 K, the data lie on a straight line with a slope of 1/2. It confirms the ESVRH mechanism and the temperature dependence of the RGO conductivity follows expression (1). As the temperature increase, a deviation of lnW from the linear behavior is observed. It indicates a transition region [45–46] at temperatures above 100 K. In the transition region, the contribution of impurity band conduction (in our case ESVRH) decreases and, at the same time, the contribution of extended band conduction increases due to the thermal excitation of charge carriers from the impurity level to the extended band [39,45–46]. A sharp increase in lnW indicates that, at temperatures above 300 K, conduction through the extended band is the dominant charge transfer mechanism in the studied RGO. It allows us to estimate the activation energy in the initial and irradiated material. The temperature dependence of conductivity at high temperatures can be described using Arrhenius behavior [7,32,39]:
(7)
σ =
σ
0 A
⋅ exp
−
E a
k B
T
where σ
0A is the prefactor different from σ
0 from (1), k
B is the Boltzmann constant, and E
a is the activation energy [32]. The value of E
a can be determined from the slope of the temperature dependence of the conductivity on plot lnσ vs 1/T. The experimental data lie in a straight line in the temperature range of 310–340 K (Fig. 6b), which confirms Arrhenius behavior. The thermally activated conductivity of the RGO at high temperatures is consistent with [7, 39, etc.]. The activation energy was calculated for the pristine and irradiated RGO with fluences of 10 × 1015 cm−2, 20 × 1015 cm−2, and 160 × 1015 cm−2. It was found that the value of E
a is about 60 meV and does not depend on the 1 MeV electron fluence (Fig. 7 ). The absence of the electron irradiation effect on the activation energy is consistent with little changes in conductivity at 300 K (Fig. 1) and unchanged average sizes of sp
2 ‑carbon crystallites (see part 3.2, Fig. 5b). The obtained value E
a ≈ 60 meV agrees with the studies [7,10,39,47], where the found activation energy lie in the range of 10–150 meV. The 60 meV activation energy has a value typical for a shallow impurity in semiconductors such as silicon, germanium [32,45,46,48].

It should be noted that the Arrhenius behavior conductivity can also be observed at nearest neighbors hopping [20,32]. But this type of hopping conduction between the nearest neighbor quantum dots is characteristic of a hypothetical array of quantum dots without disorder [20]. In our case, the studied RGO has a highly disordered structure. In addition, nearest neighbor hopping conduction in classical semiconductors is characterized by low activation energy, typically 1–10 meV. Therefore, we assume that E
a ≈ 60 meV is associated with the process of thermal excitation of carriers from the impurity localized state to the extended band. Accordingly, the activation energy E
a is close to the distance between the impurity level and the extended conduction/valence band. In other words, it is close to the impurity ionization energy.

Graphene quantum dots, like impurity atoms, probably create a band of localized states in the RGO band gap at a distance of about 60 meV from the mobility edge. Accordingly, at low temperatures (below 100 K), where thermal energy is insufficient, charge transfer occurs through the impurity band following expression (1) for ESVRH. At the temperature increase, as in crystalline semiconductors, the delocalization of carriers becomes possible due to their excitation into the extended region. Therefore, the conductivity follows expression (7) at high temperatures. In other words, in the studied temperature range of 4.2–340 K, the RGO demonstrates extrinsic conductivity due to an array of GQDs. Thus, the adjustment of the electrical properties for near-room temperature applications is possible by the properties change of the array of GQDs, which determine the impurity conductivity of the RGO.

It should be noted that the transition region between the hopping and band conductivities can be observed in a wide temperature range from ∼100 K to room temperatures and above. The existence of such a wide transition region, where it is impossible to identify the conduction mechanism, should be considered in analyzing the experimental temperature dependences to avoid misinterpretation of the experimental results. The study of the logarithmic derivative (6) can help the researcher to identify the transition region [45,46].

It should also be noted that the possibility of observing impurity band conduction (in our case ESVRH) at sufficiently high temperatures (up to 100 K) indicates the existence of a wide band gap of mobility (up to several eV) in the studied RGO. This conclusion agrees with optical studies [6–7, 10, etc.], which revealed an optical band gap in the RGO of about ∼2–4 eV. The band gap of the RGO is probably due to the disordered sp
3 ‑carbon matrix containing various functional groups and defect states. The wide band gap prevents the possibility of intrinsic conduction at room temperatures and above. Taking into account the wide band gap and the resistance of the activation energy of ∼60 meV to ionizing radiation, this indicates the possibility of using the RGO under harsh conditions. On the other hand, it should also be considered that the use of RGO is limited by a low activation energy of 0.3 eV for the transformation of electrical properties and structure in hydrogen-containing atmospheres at elevated temperatures [16].

The above studies of the conductivity at temperatures above 100 K and RGO structure in the fluence range of non-monotonic behavior allow us to make the following assumption. The modification of the electrical properties of RGO depends to a greater extent on the effect of electron irradiation on functional groups and, to a lesser extent, on the regions of crystalline sp
2 ‑carbon. Ionizing irradiation most likely has an indirect effect on the GQDs properties but through a change in the properties of the surrounding insulating matrix. The influence of the matrix is inherent in arrays of quantum dots [for example, 18–20]. The properties of quantum dots and the number of charges on them, among other things, depend on the doping of the surrounding matrix [18–20,48]. Therefore, a change in the content of oxygen functional groups, which are electrically active impurities, can probably be the reason for the increase and decrease in the conductivity of the irradiated RGO. Nevertheless, the question of sharp changes in the localization parameters in the vicinity of the fluence Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2, where a non-monotonic conductivity behavior has been revealed, remains open.

The studies of quantum dot arrays indicate that the localization length of charge carriers can be both larger and smaller than the size of quantum dots [for example, 18–20, 48]. The conductivity and localization length are affected by the density of states in the vicinity of the Fermi level as well as by the concentration of charges on QDs [18–19,48]. It is due to the unique structure of localized states in arrays of quantum dots. The studies of hopping conductivity in quantum dot arrays [for example, 18–20, 48] indicate that the density of states in the vicinity of the Fermi level has periodic peaks corresponding to the charge energy. The spacing between charging levels is e 2/(2πε
QD
ε
0
d). The peak smearing creates conditions for the appearance of a soft Coulomb gap, which causes charge transfer according to the ESVRH mechanism [18–20]. The peak smearing is due to the disorder caused by doping of the quantum dots and the surrounding isolator matrix, the disorder in size and distance between the quantum dots, and so on. Depending on the realized balance in the disordered structure, the Fermi level can be located both near the maximum of the density of states at the charge level and near the minimum between the charge levels. A shift in the Fermi level position due to structural changes leads to an increase or decrease in the localization length [18–20].

It is important to note that in our previous work [16], we reported that the localization length in RGO can be about 30 nm for the same sp
2 ‑carbon fraction (∼60 %) and the same sizes of sp
2 ‑carbon crystallites (∼6 nm) as in the current study. In other words, in RGO, the charge localization length can vary over a wide range from 1 to 30 nm, which differs from the size of graphene quantum dot (∼5–8 nm). Such behavior is consistent with our assumption that the RGO behaves like an array of GQDs immersed in an insulator matrix with a wide bandgap [18–20]. Therefore, the change in the localization length revealed in this work is probably related to the effect of electron irradiation on the density of states in the vicinity of the Fermi level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Behavior of density of states in the vicinity of Fermi level under electron irradiation
ce:section.ce:para:
The ESVRH conductivity model allows us to estimate the density of states in the vicinity of the Fermi level in the RGO under study [45,49,50]. The density of states in the vicinity of the Fermi level decreases to zero due to the Coulomb interaction of charge carriers. Thus the so-called Coulomb gap appears. The width ∆
C of the Coulomb gap is related to the dielectric constant of the studied material by the following expression [49]:
(8)
Δ C
≈
e 3
N F
1 / 2
4
πεε 0
3 / 2
where N
F is the density of states in the vicinity of the Fermi level outside the Coulomb gap.

On the other hand, the value of ∆
C can be found from the experimentally obtained temperature dependence of the conductivity by the following expression [45,49,50]:
(9)
∆ C
≈ 0.5
k B
T max
·
T 0
1 / 2
where T
max is the upper temperature, starting from which there is a deviation from the law (1). It is clear (Figs. 2a and 6a) that T
max ≈ 100 K is for pristine and irradiated samples. Fig. 8a shows the fluence dependence of the Coulomb gap width. The value of ∆C decreases from 19 meV to 15 meV with increasing fluence. Near the fluence Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2, a local minimum ∆
C ≈ 16 meV is observed. The fluence dependence of the density of states N
F is shown in Fig. 8b. In contrast to the Coulomb gap, in the vicinity of the fluence Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2, a sharp maximum of the density of states is observed. For the pristine material, N
F ∼ 1 × 1019 eV−1 cm−3, while at a fluence of Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2, the density of states increases by more than two orders of magnitude N
F ∼ 4 × 1021 eV−1 cm−3. The revealed changes in the density of localized states are consistent with the shift of the Fermi level along the periodic maxima of the density of states, which is inherent to an array of quantum dots. An increase in the density of states agrees with the conductivity increase by several times at low temperatures at a fluence of Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2 (Fig. 1). At such high values of the density of states, a transition to metallic conductivity would occur in classical semiconductors [18,51]. At the same time, the RGO is on the insulating side of IMT. It is known that the insulator-metal transition is difficult to achieve in quantum dots arrays, even at very high concentrations of charge carriers comparable to those found [18,51]. It should also be understood that the incompleteness of the theory can be the reason for the so high calculated value of the density of states. But in any case, the picture will not change qualitatively, and there is a peak in the density of states in the vicinity of the fluence Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2.

The high value of the permittivity ε = 35 can also be due to the high density of states at a fluence of Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2. An increase in the density of states from ∼1019 eV−1 cm−3 to ∼1021 eV−1 cm−3 indicates an approach to the IMT [18,32,46]. Approaching the transition is characterized by the divergence of the dielectric permittivity [18,32,46] that is consistent with the increase ε from 5 to 35. It should be considered that in classical doped semiconductors, an increase in the permittivity near MIT is accompanied by a decrease in the characteristic temperature T
0 and an increase in the localization length ξ [18,45,46,50]. In the studied RGO, the value of T
0 also demonstrates a decrease from 2000 K to 1420 K. But, at the same time, the localization length shows the opposite behavior, i.e., ξ decreases from 5 nm to 1 nm. Probably in arrays of quantum dots, in contrast to doped semiconductors, at the Fermi level shift along the peaks of the state density, the localization of charge carriers depends on several factors that can have the counter-directional effect. In any case, this issue requires additional experimental and theoretical studies.

It was shown that the studied RGO behaves like a doped semiconductor. The difference lies in the dopant, in the role of which the graphene quantum dots act that determines the unique properties. The RGO exhibits extrinsic conductivity over the entire temperature range of 4.2–340 K studied. At temperatures below 100 K, impurity conduction follows the Efros-Shklovkii variable range hopping mechanism. At temperatures above 310 K, the Arrhenius behavior is dominant due to the thermal excitation of impurity charge carriers above the mobility edge. The activation energy of about 60 meV is observed. There is a transition region in the temperature range of 100–310 K where both mechanisms act simultaneously. The possibility of observation of hopping conduction at temperatures up to 100 K indicates the presence of a wide band gap of mobility in the RGO. It is consistent with optical studies, which revealed a wide bandgap of ∼2–4 eV [6, 7, 10, etc.].

The non-monotonic behavior of the electrical properties is probably caused primarily by the interaction of ionizing irradiation with a disordered matrix. It was found that irradiation does not change the average sizes of sp
2 ‑carbon crystallites (∼6 nm) and the activation energy of conductivity at high temperatures (∼60 meV). At the same time, an effect on the content of oxygen-containing groups, which can dope or withdraw charge carriers, was found. Probably electron irradiation changes the properties of the array of graphene quantum dots and the RGO as a whole by changing the balance of disorder and doping of the surrounding matrix.

It was found that under irradiation, the non-monotonic behavior of the RGO conductivity is accompanied by non-monotonic changes in the localization parameters and the density of states. The localization length can differ several times from the size of sp
2 ‑carbon crystallites, which form an array of graphene quantum dots. In the investigated RGO before and after irradiation, the localization lengths and dielectric permittivity are within the intervals ξ ≈ 5÷7 nm and ε ≈ 5÷7, except the vicinity of the fluence Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2, corresponding to the conductivity peak. At a fluence of 10 × 1015 cm−2, the localization length decreases to ξ ≈ 1 nm, and the permittivity increases to ε ≈ 35, with the sp
2 ‑carbon domain sizes remaining unchanged (∼6 nm). It also found a peak in the density of localized states N
F in the vicinity of the Fermi level for a fluence of Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2. The density of states calculated follows the Efros-Shklovskii VRH model reaches ∼1021 eV−1 cm−3 at Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2, while at higher and lower fluences N
F ∼ 1019 eV−1 cm−3.

The non-monotonic behavior of the RGO conductivity during irradiation is probably caused by the features of the energy spectrum of the quantum dot array, which is characterized by the presence of periodic peaks in the density of states. The interaction of electron irradiation with the RGO leads to a shift of the Fermi level along the peaks of the density of states. The entry of the Fermi level into a high density of states at a fluence of Φ = 10 × 1015 cm−2 approach the RGO to the insulator-metal transition, which is consistent with the observed peak in conductivity and permittivity. The shift of the Fermi level to the region of the minimum density of states leads to a reverse change in the electrical properties. It should be noted that the decrease in the localization length from 5 nm to 1 nm observed in this work with an increase in the density of states is not typical of the insulator-metal transition. It is probably due to the simultaneous action of several counter-directional actors and requires further experimental and theoretical studies.

It is interesting to note that the final increase in the RGO conductivity after the maximum irradiation fluence of Φ = 160 × 1015 cm−2 compared with the initial material is accompanied by an increase in the oxygen content (from ∼10 % to ∼20 %) and a decrease in the sp
2 ‑carbon fraction (from ∼60 % to ∼45 %). It attracts attention to the fact that the disordered surrounding matrix is not only a barrier to the passage of current. Its role is complex, and directed modification allows to improve or adjust the properties of the RGO.
There are no funding sources.

R.M. Rudenko: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. O.O. Voitsihovska: Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. A.A. Abakumov: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. I.B. Bychko: Validation, Writing – review & editing. V.N. Poroshin: Project administration, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ synthesis and electronic transport of the carbon-coated Ag@C/MWCNT nanocomposite
